column7: message

> I don't think you understand what gender dysphoria is? It's a mismatch between a person's psychological gender identity and their physical sexual characteristics.

When will you stop redefining definitions just so it fits an agenda?

Wasn't the whole point of different brain that you were assigned the wrong sex at birth? What does gender have to do with it?

If gender was social construct like the trans community claims, why do you put social constructs in your disorder definitions? No, we have to be concise as in scientifically consise.

Gender dysphoria is the feeling you were assigned the wrong **sex**.

>When will you stop redefining definitions just so it fits an agenda?

I'm not redefining anything. I'm simply using existing definitions to attempt to describe a very complex subject which obviously there is a lot of confusion over in order to try to help people understand it.

My only agenda is to try to reduce discrimination and hatred towards trans people by attempting to address the widespread ignorance of the subject, thus improving the lives of said trans people. And you know what? If that involves changing a few definitions, so be it. Sometimes definitions need to change as our understanding of our own reality broadens, in order to adequately describe these new things.

>Wasn't the whole point of different brain that you were assigned the wrong sex at birth? What does gender have to do with it?

Well yeah that's what I'm saying. When you're classified at birth as either "male" or "female", this is generally done entirely based on the genitalia (physical sexual characteristics). This is fine for the vast majority of people whose psychological gender matches their physical sex, but for a few of us, our minds developed differently to our bodies therefore we suffer dysphoria due to this incongruence.

>If gender was social construct like the trans community claims

The trans community generally doesn't claim that. What is usually said is that gender *roles* are a social construct. But really, i depends how you define social construct. A social construct is really just a way that we, as humans, have chosen to rationalize and model the world around us, and how we communicate those ideas. So by that understanding, then yes, certain uses of the term "gender" could be considered social constructs. Does that make them any less valid? Of course not.

What's important to realize is that our socially constructed ideas of reality don't always reflect reality absolutely. They're generally things that are "good enough" for most purposes without being clouded by too much detail, given the sheer complexity of reality.

This becomes very apparent when we think about gender. At the highest, simplest level, our social construct of gender attempt to define people as either male or female. But let's compare that to reality - you soon find that problems arise when you attempt to classify people into one of these two groups based on any one particular criteria. The two classics - "Men have a penis and women have a vagina". But that doesn't work since it isn't absolute - there are intersex people born with both, there are men who have lost their penises in accidents, there are all sorts of outliers that just don't fit. So people attempt to use chromosomes - XX or XY. But again, the reality is different - there are many chromosomal abnormalities outside this scope, so again, it's not absolute, and our construct of gender fails to reflect reality.

The point is that reality is generally far, far more complex than our language allows us to convey, especially in general everyday conversation. Which brings me onto...

>why do you put social constructs in your disorder definitions? No, we have to be concise as in scientifically consise.

This is what I was attempting to do by narrowing down not just gender but *psychological* gender identity. Even this subject in itself is vastly complex and I'll never hope to even scratch the surface in a single Reddit post. So we need to make allowances - we need to simplify the subject, and come up with a "socially constructed" idea of it in order to communicate it without writing pages and pages of text. The trick is to be concise enough to convey the idea without getting too bogged down in the fine details.

So that's why I use the definitions I used - to seperate out the reality of the fact that humans can have a psychological "gender identity" that differs from their physical sexual characteristics.

I think there's a lot of value in trying to understand and communicate these complex concepts, but sometimes the definitions can get overly simplified to the point where they lose nuance. Gender dysphoria is indeed about the mismatch between one's internal sense of self and the external physical characteristics, but it's also important to recognize the broader context of how gender is understood and experienced. Simplifying it to just "being assigned the wrong sex at birth" might miss some of the deeper psychological and societal aspects. Let's aim for clarity without losing sight of the complexity.

column7: The question I ask every cis person who considers this type of question is, "why do you care what someone else considers themselves?" "What are you doing in the bathroom that you care who else is in there?" "Why do you care who that person loves and/or wants to marry?" Etc etc. Multiple questions but the premise is the same. The answers are very rarely well thought out.

> "What are you doing in the bathroom that you care who else is in there?"

Forget transgender people for a moment. Should I, a man with a too much libido and very little empathy, be allowed in the ladies showers? I would very much enjoy that.

I'm pretty sure that perverts are far more numerous than MTF transexuals, so it generally makes sense to keep anyone who has a dick out of the ladies showers, changing rooms and toilets.

Identify as whatever you like but if you've got one of those you should stay out of the ladies, and if you've not then it's probably in your best interest to stay out of the gents because it's full of perverts in there.

I think we need to focus on respect and safety for everyone, not just assumptions based on anatomy. Transgender individuals face enough discrimination; let's not add to it by suggesting they shouldn't use certain facilities.

Not really but fine, I'll play along.

1. Clintons allegations were 20 years ago. The world has obviously changed. More women have accused Roy Moore and Donald Trump and their defences have been terrible, if that. Plus he wasn't accused during his campaign.

2. Rent control isn't the whole point of the democratic agenda. The bult of the current tax bill is almost entirely panned by economists. Having one policy disliked by economists isn't the same as your whole platform being bad for the economy.

column7:
> While market rates should generally be the ruling factor, at some point paying someone so little that they can't live on their salary is exploitation. You are taking advantage of a bad position that someone is in to extract labor from them. It's borderline coercive.

How is this different from the price of a house going down when there's a surplus of buyers?  Am I exploiting homeowners by waiting until prices turn in my favor to buy their house from them?  When someone has a more immediate need to sell their house (get a job), then the price it takes to buy it from them naturally goes down.

That's not exploitation.  It's supply and demand.

I think the main kinds of reasoning behind minimum wages include:

1) While market rates should generally be the ruling factor, at some point paying someone so little that they can't live on their salary is exploitation. You are taking advantage of a bad position that someone is in to extract labor from them. It's borderline coercive. 

Personally, I think that's a poor reason to prevent someone from being hired, but I can't shake the niggling feeling that employment is not the 100% voluntary thing that people like to claim it is. If the choice is work in unfair conditions or literally die, you'll do what you have to.

2) Businesses that pay particularly low wages (below minimum survival level) are taking advantage of taxpayers as well, because of the social support services that they are paying for. 

Ultimately, it's about exploitation and effective coercion (not direct and literal, but profiting from desparation).

column7: @chromatic-belt I completely agree that paying someone so little they can't afford basic necessities is exploitation. It's not just about supply and demand; it's about human dignity and basic rights. @associative-error I understand where you're coming from, but I think we need to recognize that many people don't have the luxury of choosing between fair and unfair conditions. Survival often forces people into situations they wouldn't otherwise choose.

I was specifically refering to the entire premise the Black lives Matter movement is built on.

Which is a myth.

Cops shoot unarmed WHITES disproportionately.

As for why cops stop and question black youths more, it is in large part because of environment, "high crime" neighborhoods. Unfortunately a larger portion of violent crimes and drug offenses occur in low income neighborhoods. There is also a disproportionate number of black people who live there.

As for the Vice video... I've been pulled over before, the officer ALWAYS asks "is this your car". I think people are looking to be offended because they've been told they should be offended. Their reaction to that offense causes tension that escalates the situation.

Is that true in every case?

No of coarse not, there's some dirty cops, there are some horrible racist cops. Just as there are horrible racist people in all walks of life. But I think the offended  "victim" mentality plays a large role, and this results in a self fulfilling prophecy.

I find it frustrating when people dismiss real issues just to fit their narrative. Police shootings of unarmed individuals, regardless of race, are a serious concern that needs addressing. We shouldn't ignore the disproportionate impact on any group. And while environmental factors play a role, it doesn't excuse the systemic issues within law enforcement. Let's focus on solutions rather than shifting blame.
